coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterisation of dual sensing carbon dots for water and Cu2+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Chunyu
Zhang, Danyang
Zhu, Qi
Chao, Daiyong
Liu, Hua
Zhou, Liang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, hydroquinone, o-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid were used as reaction precursors to synthesize carbon dots (h-CDs) by a one-step solvothermal method. Our experimental results demonstrated the as-prepared functionalized h-CDs have not only excellent optical properties but also interestingly chemical and physical properties. The emission peak of the obtained h-CDs shifts gradually towards the red region with increasing water content in organic solvent. This was visually manifest as a change in color. Therefore, the h-CDs can be used as the colorimetric fluorescent probe to detect water content in organic solvents. Moreover, it is also particularly sensitive to Cu2+ and thus could be used as fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ content in water with detection limit of 1.8 × 10−4 mmol. This investigation provides a new way for the development of carbon dots (CDs) as multifunctional fluorescent probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Water content
Cu2+ content
Fluorescent probe
Detection lower limit

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram for the preparation route of the h-CDs. (b) TEM image of the obtained h-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the h-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s and (d) N 1s of the h-CDs.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of h-CDs dispersed in acetone solution excited at 380–450 nm wavelength. (b) UV–vis absorption (black line) and fluorescence emission (red line, excitation wavelength of 400 nm). (c) UV–vis absorption spectra, (d) Fluorescence spectra and (e) Fluorescence photos of h-CDs dispersed in acetone with different water contents (0–100%).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of h-CDs solutions with Cu2+ contents of 0–0.01 mmol. (b) The linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity ((F–F0)/F0) and Cu2+ concentration (0–0.01 mmol). (c) Fluorescence spectra of a mixture of various metal ions (0.05 mmol) with 0.01 mmol Cu2+. (d) Relative fluorescence intensities of the h-CDs towards the various interfering metal ions (0.05 mmol) in the absence and presence of 0.01 mmol Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterisation of dual sensing carbon dots for water and Cu2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Advanced Optoelectronic Functional Materials Research, And Key Laboratory of UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, Jilin. PR China

And Key Laboratory of UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, hydroquinone, o-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid were used as reaction precursors to synthesize carbon dots (h-CDs) by a one-step solvothermal method. Our experimental results demonstrated the as-prepared functionalized h-CDs have not only excellent optical properties but also interestingly chemical and physical properties. The emission peak of the obtained h-CDs shifts gradually towards the red region with increasing water content in organic solvent. This was visually manifest as a change in color. Therefore, the h-CDs can be used as the colorimetric fluorescent probe to detect water content in organic solvents. Moreover, it is also particularly sensitive to Cu2+ and thus could be used as fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ content in water with detection limit of 1.8 × 10−4 mmol. This investigation provides a new way for the development of carbon dots (CDs) as multifunctional fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:para: In the chemical production of anhydrous solvent, moisture sensitive chemicals and petrochemical petroleum products, the presence of water as common pollutant and impurity is usually annoying and even dangerous [1–4]. In addition, the characterization of water content in medicine, chemical industry, food and other fields is also an important index [5]. Therefore, it is distinctly significant to accurately and rapidly measure the moisture content in organic products during the experimental processes. In addition, how to quickly and accurately detect the content of Cu2+ is another important task because excessive Cu2+ in water will kill aquatic organisms and destroy the ecological balance, and even endanger people's health [6–9].

The chemosensor has received wide attention and application because of its fast, high sensitivity and no need for a monochromator. In traditional detection and analysis strategies, a large number of natural enzymes are usually used as catalysts to achieve high-sensitivity detection [10]. Or use sensitive approaches such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [11], Karl-Fischer titration [12] and anodic stripping voltammetry have been applied to detect the content of a substance in various samples. Although they allow sensitive detection of water or heavy metal ions in the samples, cost issue, selectivity, environmental friendliness and difficulty in synthesis are sometimes problematic. In addition, in order to sensitively detect the impurity content in the sample, it is necessary to additionally use organic derivatization reagents such as fluorescein [13], or use sophisticated experimental equipment such as atomic absorption spectroscopy and nuclear magnetic resonance and well-trained personnel [14]. With the development of research, the method using fluorescent sensors shows outstanding properties, such as simple operation, short response time, and highly sensitivity. In the application of method using fluorescent sensors, it is important to design a fluorescent material. CDs have attracted great attention due to due to its advantages of low cost [15,16], low toxicity [17–19] and good water solubility [1,20–22]. Previous investigations have demonstrated that CDs as fluorescent probes play important roles in detecting water content in organic solvents and heavy metal ions content in water. For example, Wang et al. prepared ratiometric fluorescent CDs probe and used it to determine the water content in organic solvents [23]. Pawar's group designed and synthesized a novel nanoprobe by functioning chitosan CDs with a modified bipyridine heterocyclic molecule 4-(pyridine-2-yl)-3H-pyrrolo[2,3-c]quinoline to detect trace water [24]. Wei et al. also established a simple and direct method to prepare CDs for the detection of water contents in organic solvents [25]. Shahamirifard et al. first reported the application of CDs as complexant and sensitizer in constructing an optical sensor, which has been successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ content in water samples [26]. Bhati et al. obtained a red CDs probe with good biocompatibility by simple carbonization of leaf extracts, which can be used for fluorescence selective detection of Cu2+ [27]. Guo's group successfully synthesized a new composite material based on CDs and zeolite imidazole acid skeleton, and the synthesized CDs can be used as a sensitive ‘on-off’ fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ in the off mode [7]. However, these reported CDs have generally only single detection function as fluorescent probes, and it will be of great significance to develop the functionalization of CDs as much as possible.

In response, our group has developed h-CDs that can be used as fluorescent probes directly for visual and quantitative detection of water in different organic solvents [28]. On that basis, we report the synthesis of fluorescent h-CDs from hydroquinone, o-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid as precursors by a one-step solvothermal method. After being diluted with acetone, the synthesized h-CDs exhibited strong green emission under UV-light excitation, which is sensitive to water in organic solvents. With increasing water content in acetone, the emission peak shift gradually towards red region. Based on this discovery, the new h-CDs can be used as sensitive sensor for water content in organic solvents. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the obtained h-CDs was also sensitive to Cu2+ content in the water. For acetone solution of the obtained h-CDs with water content of 40%, with the addition of Cu2+, the emission intensity decreased gradually. This phenomenon revealed the obtained h-CDs can be used as fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ concentration.

Sodium hydroxide, sulfuric acid, hydroquinone, o-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethanol, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, methyl alcohol, ferric chloride (FeCl3), copper chloride (CuCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), Sodium chloride (NaCl), manganese chloride (MnCl2), potassium chloride (KCl) and cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Works. Lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), barium carbonate (BaCO3), zinc chloride (ZnCl2) and Chromic nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3–9H2O) were obtained from Xilong Chemical Co. Cadmium acetate (C2H4CdO2) and mercury chloride (HgCl2) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. All of the chemicals used in the experiments are analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The deionized water used was not further refined.

Hydroquinone (0.1 g), o-phenylenediamine (0.1 g) and terephthalic acid (0.1 g) was dissolved in ethanol (10 mL), the solution was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene lined autoclave (25 mL), which was heated to 180 °C and kept for 12 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature. To remove the larger particles, the crude products were centrifuged (12 000 rpm) and then filtered using 0.22 μm filter membrane. The obtained supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (molecular weight of 7000 Da) for 12 h to remove the larger molecules. Finally, the h-CDs powder was obtained by drying the purified solution in vacuum oven. 40-CDs (The h-CDs was dispersed in an acetone solution with a water content of 40%) powder was also prepared according to the above method.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with FEI Tecnai G2F20 microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected using Nicolet-6700 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained with PHI 5000 ULVAC-PHI Versa Probe. UV–vis absorption spectra were collected using Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Fluorescent emission spectra were recorded on Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Absolute Quantum Yield (QY) was obtained employing a C-9920-02 spectrometer. Raman spectra were measured using Horiba JobinYvon T64000. Inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP) was performed on a TJA ICP-MS X2 system.

For sensing of Cu2+, 10 ml aliquots (6 ml of acetone and 4 ml of water) of containing h-CDs (2 × 10−5 g/mL) and Cu2+ ions (0–0.01 mmol) were incubated at 25 °C for 12 h prior to fluorescence measurements. To further explore the selectivity of the fluorescent h-CDs for sensing metal ions, the suspensions (The ratio of acetone to water is 3:2) containing h-CDs (2 × 10−5 g/mL), Cu2+ (0.01 mmol) were prepared. The fluorescent spectra of the suspensions were recorded by adding a series of metal ions (Ba2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+ and Zn2+) with the same concentration (0.05 mmol). For the fluorescence measurement, the excitation wavelength was set at 400 nm. 0.05 mmol various metal ions were prepared by adding separately NaCl (0.058 g), HgCl2 (0.271 g), C2H4CdO2 (0.172 g), MgCl2 (0.095 g), KCl (0.075 g), MnCl2 (0.198 g), ZnCl2 (0.136 g), FeCl3 (0.27 g), CoCl2·6H2O (0.238 g), Pb(NO3)2 (0.331 g), BaCO3 (0.197 g) and Cr(NO3)3–9H2O (0.4 g) to 1000 mL of deionized water, then the solutions of the metal ions were diluted 20 times. While 0.01 mmol Cu2+ was prepared by adding CuCl2 (0.134 g) to 1000 mL of deionized water, then the solution was diluted 100 times.

The h-CDs, which are readily soluble in organic solvents were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method (Fig. 1 a). The acetone solution of the obtained h-CDs exhibited strong green fluorescence with an absolute photoluminescence QY up to 30.8% (Fig. S1, in Supporting Information) under 400 nm wavelength excitation. According to the TEM picture (Fig. 1b), the size distribution of h-CDs was mainly in the range of 4.5–6.5 nm uniformly with an average size of 5.0 nm and distribution without obvious aggregation. The XRD pattern (Fig. S2, in Supporting Information) of the h-CDs contains two emission peaks at (002) and (101), 23° and 45°, respectively, which confirms the graphite-like structure of the h-CDs [29,30].

In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a), the saturated carbon characteristic aromatic C–H stretching vibrations at 3000-2768 cm−1 is also observed. An absorption peak appears between 1960 and 1650 cm−1. The stretching vibration of CC/CN occurs in the region of 1675–1500 cm−1. In this band region, the CC skeleton vibratory of mononuclear aromatic hydrocarbons presents characteristic absorption peaks of two peaks at 1600 and 1500 cm−1, respectively. The spectra information in the band of 1300–1000 cm−1 is mainly the stretching vibration region of C–O [31–33]. We have further analyzed the surface information of h-CDs with the help of XPS. As presented in Fig. S3, in Supporting Information, there are three typical peaks of C 1s (284.6 eV), O 1s (532.2 eV) and N 1s (399.0 eV), respectively. In the C 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b), there are three distinct peaks at 284.4, 286.6 and 289.8 eV, which correspond to C–N/C–O, CO/CN and C(O)–O, respectively [28,34]. Fig. 2c shows the high resolution O 1s spectrum of h-CDs, the two main peaks at 531.6 and 535.0 eV could be attributed to CO/CN and C–O, respectively. In the case of N 1s (Fig. 2d), there are three peaks at 398.3, 399.5 and 400.6 eV attributed to pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively [35]. The XPS results coincide with the FT-IR data. These indicate that we have successfully synthesized h-CDs with rich –OH and –COOH groups on the surface, which not only make h-CDs more hydrophilic but also strengthen their binding with Cu2+ [36,37].

As described in Fig. S4, in Supporting Information, Raman spectra of the obtained h-CDs contain two distinct peaks centered at around 1375 and 1564 cm−1, which are assigned to a D band arising from vibrations of disordered carbon atoms and a G band arising from the sp2-conjugated carbon atoms, respectively. The peak intensity ratio of D (1360 cm−1) and G (1580 cm−1) bands (ID/IG) was calculated to be 0.96, which suggested the presence of graphite structure in h-CDs [38–40]. On the other hand, the high intensity of D band as well as the high width of both D and G bands indicates the existence of high-density defects [33,41].

The absorption spectrum of the obtained h-CDs dissolved in acetone solution has two obvious peaks at 419 and 437 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 a), which can be attributed to the ‘surface bands’ formed due to the presence of abundant chromophores on the surface of h-CDs. It can be observed in Fig. 3b that the position of emission peak does not change with the change of excitation wavelength. It means the synthesized h-CDs show excitation independent emission characteristics. Fig. 3c displays the UV–vis absorption spectra of the obtained h-CDs dispersed in acetone with different water contents. The absorption peak of the obtained h-CDs shifts gradually towards the red region with increasing water content in acetone. With increasing water content, as shown in Figs. 3d and S5 (in Supporting Information), the maximum emission peak shifted gradually from 484 to 563 nm under the same excitation wavelength, thus resulting in the change of emission color from blue to yellow (Fig. 3e). The phenomenon can be interpreted as the presence of water, which changes the surface state of the h-CDs in acetone. The fluorescent emission of the h-CDs was strongly associated with the content of CN functional groups on their surface. It can be observed from Fig. 2b and Fig. S6a (in in Supporting Information) that the contents of CN rises with increasing addition of water. The band gap narrows with increasing CN content on the h-CDs surface. Increasing the number of CN on the h-CDs surface introduces more new energy levels into the electronic structures of the h-CDs, resulting in more electronic transitions and thus a red shift of the fluorescent [42].

We take the h-CDs acetone solution with 40% water as example. The XPS spectrum was also collected to analyze the surface compositions of the 40-CDs. As shown in Fig. S3, in Supporting Information, there are three main peaks at C 1s (284.6 eV), O 1s (532.4 eV) and N 1s (398.8 eV), respectively. The C 1s band (Fig. S6a in Supporting Information) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.5, 286.6 and 289.9 eV, representing C–N/C–O, CO/CN and C(O)–O, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S6b in Supporting Information) contains two peaks at 531.8 and 535.6 eV, representing CO and C–O–H, respectively. In the N 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. S6c in Supporting Information), three peaks at 398.2 (pyridinic N), 399.3 (amino N) and 400.8 eV (pyrrolic N), respectively, can be observed. In contrast, the presence of water in 40-CDs solution increases the C–O content slightly. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2a, the peak of C–O value increases to 1300-1000 cm−1, indicates that the presence of water changes the surface state of h-CDs and increases the surface C–O content. The results of XPS analysis are consistent with those of FT-IR.

According to formula (D% = 3σ/k, σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank samples and k is the slope of the first function fitting the change of peak position of water content from 0 to 100%), the detection lower limit of water content in acetone using h-CDs as fluorescent probe is calculated as 0.73%. The optical properties of h-CDs in three other solvents (methanol, ethanol and DMF) were further investigated and shown in Fig. S7, in Supporting Information. According to our experimental results, the detection lower limits of water content in methanol, ethanol and DMF are 0.53%, 0.66% and 0.75%, respectively.

Simultaneously, the obtained h-CDs were also used as fluorescent probe to detect the content of Cu2+ in water solution. Firstly, the obtained h-CDs were dissolved into acetone. Then, the acetone solution containing h-CDs were mixed with the aqueous solution containing Cu2+ in a ratio of 3:2. Compared with h-CDs acetone solution contains 40% water, as shown in Fig. 4 a, the presence of Cu2+ reduces the luminous intensity of h-CDs in some degree. This phenomenon can be interpreted as that the surface structures and chemical bonds of h-CDs are destroyed due to the addition of Cu2+, which combine with the surface bond of h-CDs and form a non-fluorescent ground state complex containing Cu. With increasing addition of Cu2+, the luminous intensity of h-CDs decreases rapidly and finally disappears when the content of Cu2+ is close to 0.01 mmol (Fig. S8, in Supporting Information). This result confirms that h-CDs have relatively higher detection sensitivity for Cu2+. More importantly, this phenomenon of fluorescence quenching is irreversible. With increasing addition of Cu2+, as shown in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2a), the absorption intensity of the absorption peaks at 1760-1200 cm−1 decreases obviously. While the absorption peak of C–O between 1200 and 920 cm−1 increases significantly. This phenomenon demonstrates that Cu2+ destroy the original C–H and CC/CN and then convert to C–O·Cu+. In this regard, we further measured the XPS spectrum of Cu-CDs (h-CDs with a Cu2+ content of 0.01 mmol), as shown in Fig. S9, in Supporting Information. Experimental data indicated that C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and Cu 2p signals appeared at 287.5, 531.2, 399.6 and 933.8 eV, respectively (Fig. S3, in Supporting Information). It is worth noting that a new peak value of 530.5 eV was observed in the high resolution O 1s spectrum of Cu-CDs (Fig. S9b, in Supporting Information), indicating that Cu2+ is not simply mixed with h-CDs but formed C–O·Cu+. Meanwhile, as shown in the XPS high resolution images of C 1s (Fig. 2b, Fig. S6a and Fig. S9a, in Supporting Information), the contents of C–O decreases with increasing addition of Cu2+. In the high resolution XPS spectrum of Cu-CDs, as shown in Fig. S9d (in Supporting Information), the peaks at 932.0, 934.2 and 953.7 eV correspond to Cu+ 2p3/2, Cu2+ 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively [10,34]. This means that Cu is involved in Cu-CDs and corresponds to the high resolution C 1s as well as O 1s spectra, which confirms our above hypothesis. After the addition of Cu2+, as shown in Fig. S4, in Supporting Information, the intensities of D and G peaks in the Raman spectrum of Cu-CDs were significantly reduced, while the calculated intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the two peaks rose from 0.96 to 1.17 due to the faster decrease of G peak. This result indicated the presence of energy transfer and electron transfer processes between Cu2+ and h-CDs. At the same time, Cu2+ destroy the inter-structures and reduce sp2 hybridization sites, thus leading to decreased fluorescence intensity.

Fluorescence intensities of h-CDs solutions were measured and compared by adjusting the pH from 1 to 14, as shown in Fig. S11a, in Supporting Information. To intuitively explain the influence of pH on fluorescence intensity of h-CDs, we processed these data as shown in Fig. S11b, in Supporting Information, where I
0 was the fluorescence intensity at pH of 7, I was the fluorescence intensity of h-CDs at different pH. Under the condition of strong acid or strong alkali, the fluorescence of h-CDs is quenched significantly because amino group, carboxyl group and other functional groups on the surface of h-CDs are protonated and deprotonated, which leads to the change in surface charges and the rapid response of h-CDs to strong acid or strong alkali [43,44]. Interestingly, fluorescence intensity is basically stable when pH was within the range of 4–10. Therefore, if the h-CDs are used to detect Cu2+ content in rivers, lakes and other natural water sources with different pH conditions, test results will hardly suffer from the variation of pH. This confirms that our h-CDs can adapt to a variety of acidic or alkaline complex environments, which is helpful in minimizing the detection inaccuracy caused by different water qualities.

Based on the above analysis, the detection of Cu2+ content using h-CDs as fluorescent probe was further explored. To confirm the selectivity of h-CDs in detecting Cu2+, the effect of following ions: Ba2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ on the luminescence properties of h-CDs were also studied and compared in Fig. 4c and d and Fig. S12, in Supporting Information. These results indicate the obtained h-CDs are highly selective for Cu2+, as the h-CDs solution with Cu2+ show significantly lower fluorescence intensity compared with solutions with other ions. Under the excitation of 400 nm ultraviolet lamp, as shown in Fig. S13, in Supporting Information, Cu2+ solution show nearly invisible fluorescence while other ionic solutions show still strong fluorescence. Meanwhile, the color of the solutions containing Cu2+ changed significantly in sunlight. Fig. 4b shows that fluorescence emission intensity was quenched linearly over a fixed concentration range of Cu2+, which indicates the h-CDs can be used for the quantitative determination of Cu2+, and the detection lower limit was calculated to be 1.8 × 10−4 mmol. In order to further evaluate the practicability of h-CDs, it is used for the determination of actual water samples. Three groups of water samples were taken from Changchun South Lake, tap water, and purified drinking water, and measured with an ICP and found that the contents of Cu2+ contained in them was below the detection limit of the method (Fig. S14, in Supporting Information). Three groups of water samples were spiked with Cu2+ at a concentration of 3 × 10−3, 6 × 10−3, 9 × 10−3 mmol/L, and the results showed that the recovery rate was between 97.6% and 115% (Table S1, in Supporting Information), and the relative standard deviation is between 2.19% and 4.45%. It indicate that this method has good analytical performance and can be applied to actual water samples.

In summary, this work focuses on the application of h-CDs synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method as dual sensing fluorescent probes in the content detection of water and Cu2+. With increasing water content in organic solvents, the fluorescence emission peak of the obtained h-CDs shifts gradually towards red region. With increasing Cu2+ content in different solutions, the fluorescence emission intensity decreases rapidly. And the synthesized carbon dots have good optical properties, which can be used for the detection of Cu2+ in actual water samples. This investigation provides not only a new basis for the quantitative determination of water and Cu2+ contents in organic solvent but also a new idea for the preparation of multifunctional fluorescent CDs.

Chunyu Yu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, preparation, Writing – review & editing. Danyang Zhang: Data curation. Qi Zhu: Investigation. Daiyong Chao: Formal analysis. Hua Liu: Methodology, Supervision. Liang Zhou: Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial aid from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21771172), Research Equipment Development Project of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQZ20200005), and Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. XDB20000000).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Smart carbon dots as chemosensor for control of water contamination in organic media
Sensor Actuator B Chem

Dalton Trans

Anal Chem

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of a carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 composite for enhanced Cu(2+)sensing
